explanations.19: This tells bash to immediately stop running if any command fails or if you use a variable that was never set, which prevents silent errors.
explanations.44: The trap command sets up a function to automatically run when the script exits, like a cleanup routine that always happens at the end.
explanations.48: This checks if LOG_FILE was never set before, and if so, creates a temporary log file with a random name in /tmp.
explanations.51: This defines a small helper function inside the main function so it can be used later; it just gets the current date and time.
explanations.60: BASH_LINENO is a special array that tracks which line each function was called from; this gets its length.
explanations.62: This loop starts at index 2 because index 0 is the trace function itself and index 1 is the write function, so we skip those.
explanations.63: FUNCNAME is an array of all the function names in the call stack, and BASH_LINENO has the line numbers; this builds a string showing the chain of function calls.
explanations.66: This removes the trailing semicolon from the trace string using sed, a text replacement tool.
explanations.74: This opens file descriptor 3 (a way to write to a file) and points it to the log file, so later >&3 writes go to that file.
explanations.98: [[ -s ]] means the file exists AND has content; this checks both conditions at once.
explanations.141: This creates the log file if it doesn't exist yet, but skips creation if LOG_FILE is set to /dev/stdout (which is already writable).
explanations.154: The >&3 redirects the output to file descriptor 3, which was set up to point to the log file.
explanations.184: This sets the text color to gray using ANSI codes; the \033[ and m are color instructions for the terminal.
explanations.185: This sed command removes ANSI color codes from the log file before displaying it, so you see clean text without the color control characters.
explanations.195: This clever trick checks if the script was sourced (included) or run directly by trying to return from the subshell; return only works in sourced scripts.
